title: The tag wants details
why.no-lang: There is no language attribute. It is nowhere visible — but Google reads it to know who to show the page to, and screen readers use it so they do not read one language with another's accent.
why.wrong-place: The attribute is there, but not on the right tag. The language belongs to the whole page, not a part of it — so it lives on the opening <html>.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed.
blocks.0.text: So far your tags said one thing: what this is. A heading. A paragraph. A list. Sometimes, though, that is not enough.
blocks.1.text: Soon you will want a link. "This is a link" — fine, but where does it lead? Or an image. "This is an image" — which one exactly?
blocks.4.text: It lives inside the opening tag. It has a name (title), then an equals sign, then a value in quotes. Quotes are mandatory.
blocks.5.text: Put this attribute on the sentence on the right, run, then hover with your mouse and wait a second. A little bubble appears.
blocks.6.text: You have already seen one attribute
blocks.8.text: There it is: `charset` is the name, `UTF-8` is the value. You just did not know what the thing was called back then.
blocks.11.text: It says what language the page is in. It is nowhere visible — but Google reads it to know who to show the page to, and screen readers use it so they do not read one language with another's accent.
blocks.12.text: Put it on your page (`bg` for Bulgarian, `en` for English).
blocks.13.text: The tag says what a thing is. The attribute says the details about it. Everything from here on in HTML is a combination of the two.
